coredata.dc:title: Boron and nitrogen codoped carbon dots as fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ with improved selectivity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Hao
Pang, Lan-Fang
Fu, Meng-Jie
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are popular as fluorescence sensors, and metal ions are typical analytes. However, CDs used as fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ have some interferences coming from co-existed ions. In this study, we suspect that sp3 boron atom in phenylboronic acid group will be more compatible with Fe3+ to form coordination bonds, thereby increasing the selectivity to Fe3+. Hence, we designed and synthesized boron and nitrogen codoped carbon dots (BN-CDs) for detection of Fe3+ via a hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine (OPA) and 4-(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolan-2-yl)benzylchloroformate as precursors. From the results, we found that BN-CDs had superior selectivity to Fe3+ in the presence of the other common interfering metal ions like Cu2+, Fe2+ and Pb2+. Besides, the obtained BN-CDs exhibited good water solubility, favorable photostability, excellent pH stability between pH 2–11, and strong fluorescence intensity with quantum yield up to 31.5 %. These excellent properties of carbon dots validate that our idea is feasible, and can be used for design CDs for Fe3+ detection. Quenching mechanism study showed the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs could be dramatically quenched by Fe3+ through dynamic and static synergy process. Finally, the as prepared BN-CDs were successfully applied to the determination of Fe3+ in fetal bovine serum and lake water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+ detection
Boron
Nitrogen
Fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology Characterization of BN-CDs. (A) TEM images of BN-CDs. Insert: HRTEM of BN-CDs (B) Size distribution of BN-CDs. (C) AFM images of BN-CDs. (D) Height profiles analysis of Fig. 1C (red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FTIR spectra of BN-CDs and the precursors. Top: BN-CDs. Below: Boron-containing precursors.
(A) XPS survey spectra of BN-CDs. (B–D) Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of BN-CDs. (B) C1s, (C): N1s, (D): B1s.
Normalized UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs and the precursors.
Fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs versus different pH values in 0.1 M of citrate buffer solutions.

Quenching coefficient of BN-CDs versus 200 μM of Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, Hg2+ and K+. Black: without Fe3+. Red: adding 200 μM of Fe3+. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The quenching efficiency of BN-CDs versus Fe3+ concentrations. (A) 0.5–80 μM. (B) 80–200 μM. F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of BN-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence intensity decay curve of BN-CDs. (B) The quenching efficiency of BN-CDs at different temperature. F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of BN-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. (C) Illustration of quenching mechanism of BN-CDs by Fe3+. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of other reported CDs for Fe3+ detection by this work.
Analysis results of lake water samples and fetal bovine serum samples.

head.ce:title: Boron and nitrogen codoped carbon dots as fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ with improved selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron and nitrogen codoped carbon dots (BN-CDs) was prepared via a hydrothermal method, and their strong fluorescence could be quenched by Fe3+ ions selectively. These BN-CDs were successfully applied to detect Fe3+ in lake water and fetal bovine serum.

Carbon dots (CDs) are popular as fluorescence sensors, and metal ions are typical analytes. However, CDs used as fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ have some interferences coming from co-existed ions. In this study, we suspect that sp3 boron atom in phenylboronic acid group will be more compatible with Fe3+ to form coordination bonds, thereby increasing the selectivity to Fe3+. Hence, we designed and synthesized boron and nitrogen codoped carbon dots (BN-CDs) for detection of Fe3+ via a hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine (OPA) and 4-(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolan-2-yl)benzylchloroformate as precursors. From the results, we found that BN-CDs had superior selectivity to Fe3+ in the presence of the other common interfering metal ions like Cu2+, Fe2+ and Pb2+. Besides, the obtained BN-CDs exhibited good water solubility, favorable photostability, excellent pH stability between pH 2–11, and strong fluorescence intensity with quantum yield up to 31.5 %. These excellent properties of carbon dots validate that our idea is feasible, and can be used for design CDs for Fe3+ detection. Quenching mechanism study showed the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs could be dramatically quenched by Fe3+ through dynamic and static synergy process. Finally, the as prepared BN-CDs were successfully applied to the determination of Fe3+ in fetal bovine serum and lake water.

ce:section.ce:para: As an essential semi-micronutrient in human body, ferric iron (Fe3+) plays a vital role in body regulation including pathology and pathophysiology process. For example, deficiency of Fe3+ can induce iron deficiency anemia (IDA) [1], reduce the activity of iron enzyme in body, and result in metabolic disorders. Accumulation of excess Fe3+ can also cause serious diseases such as hepatic and renal injury, Parkinsonism, Alzheimer's disease, and cancers [2]. In addition, the content of Fe3+ ions in environmental water is an important indicator for monitoring water pollution [3]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop sensitive analytical techniques for detecting Fe3+ in biological and environmental samples.

As a novel carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial, CDs have aroused extensive attention due to their optical and chemical properties [4]. Compared to other fluorescent materials, CDs have outstanding merits including good water solubility, favorable biocompatibility, tunable surface functionalities, and good stability against photo bleaching. Besides, the preparation of CDs is simple, cheap and easy to operate [5], and CDs are popular as new nanomaterials for fluorescence detection. However, although these CDs are easy to prepare, their surface functional groups are often uncontrollable. Therefore, when these CDs are used as fluorescent sensors, they often face shortcomings such as poor selectivity, severe coexistence interference, and obvious pH effects. One of the common analytes of CDs fluorescent sensors is Fe3+, and such drawbacks are common in related literature. Bai et al. [6] reported CDs doped with nitrogen and silicon for Fe3+ detection. These CDs had multi-excitation and single color emission, and can be internalized into live cells with obvious accumulation in cell nuclei. But the selectivity experiments showed that Cu2+ and Hg2+ would cause severe interference for Fe3+ detection. Ahn et al. [7] reported highly fluorescent N-doped CDs synthesized from food waste via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. These CDs led extraordinary fluorescent performance with high quantum yield of 28 %, and showed good cell viability for bio-imaging, but Fe2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ caused interference when these CDs were applied as fluorescence probe for Fe3+. Such defects make it difficult to guarantee the reliability of the obtained results.

The B atom in the boric acid group is easy to combine with the hydroxyl group to form a sp3 hybrid B atom. After the oxygen atom is combined, the boric acid group contains three oxygen atoms that can be coordinated. We suspect that these three oxygen atoms will be more compatible with Fe3+ to form coordination bonds, thereby increasing the selectivity to Fe3+. Thus, we prepared boron and nitrogen co-doped CDs (BN-CDs) with boric acid groups on the surface. BN-CDs could be obtained using 4-(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolan-2-yl)benzylchloroformate and o-phenylenediamine (OPA) as precursors. The as prepared BN-CDs showed a good pH stability and an excellent selectivity for Fe3+ with the coexist of Cu2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, Hg2+ and K+. Besides, the quantum yield of the BN-CDs was up to 31.5 %, which was higher than many of the reported Fe3+ responsive fluorescence CDs. The experimental results also verified our above conjecture. It is worth noting that the quenching coefficient ((F0-F)/F0) of BN-CDs showed a good liner relationship versus Fe3+ concentration in the liner range of 0.5–80 μM (R2 = 0.996) and 80–200 μM (R2 = 0.995), with detection limit (LOD) of 0.1 μM (S/N = 3). Finally, the as prepared BN-CDs were successfully applied to detect Fe3+ in the fetal bovine serum and local lake samples.

Synthesis of 4-(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolan-2-yl)benzylchloroformate (compound 1) was according literature [8]. Anhydrous Na2CO3 (3.5 g, 33.0 mmol) was added to a round-bottom flack. After the flask was cooled to 0 °C, triphosgene (2.2 g, 7.4 mmol) in anhydrous toluene (15 mL) was added slowly. The mixture was stirred for 0.5 h at 0 °C, then 4-(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolan-2-yl)benzylalcohol (0.87 g, 3.7 mmol) in anhydrous toluene (5 mL) was added and stirred for 6 h at 25 °C. The crude product after usual work-up was used without further purification; 1H NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz): δ 7.84 (d, J =8.1 Hz, 2 H) 7.39 (dd, J = 8.1, 8.1 Hz, 2H), 5.31 (s, 2H), 1.35 (s, 12H). Preparation of BN-CDs: OPA (0.54 g, 5.0 mmol) and 1 (0.59 g, 2.0 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of THF. After that, catalytic amount of N,N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) was added to the solution and stirred for 8 h at 25 °C. The insoluble matter was filtered out and the solvent was distilled under vacuum. The obtained mixture of the above crude product (0.5 g) was dissolved in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol with ultrasonication, and then the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon autoclave reactor and heated for 16 h at 200 °C. After cooled to room temperature, the product was filtered with a 0.22 μm filtration membrane and then dialyzed against ethanol for 24 h with a regenerated cellulose dialysis membrane (1000 Da). The solvent of dialysate was evaporated and the product was dried in vacuum for 24 h and then stored at 4 °C.

Tryptophan (Φ R = 0.13) was chosen as the reference for QYs measurements [9], The QYs of BN-CDs were calculated using the following equation:
(1)
Φ S = (Φ RODRISηS 2)/(ODSIRηR 2)

R and S referred to Tryptophan and the BN-CDs, respectively; Φ was the QYs, OD was the optical density measured by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, I was the integrated intensity measured by fluorescence spectroscopy, η was the refractive index. The obtained BN-CDs and tryptophan were dissolved in deionized water. Both of their concentrations were regulated to keep their optical densities between 0.01 and 0.1. Optical densities were measured at 280 nm; both the solutions of BN-CDs and tryptophan were excited at 280 nm.

The photostability of BN-CDs was assessed under an 8 W ultraviolet lamp irradiation at 365 nm for 24 h, and the fluorescence intensities were recorded at 3 h, 6 h, 9 h, 12 h, 15 h, 24 h. pH stability was further investigated using citric acid-sodium citrate buffer (0.1 M). To study the pH dependence on fluorescence behavior, 1 mg of BN-CDs was first dissolved in 1 mL of ethanol. After that 50 μL of the above solution was diluted to 5 mL by 0.1 M citric acid-sodium citrate buffer, and the fluorescence intensities of the obtained solutions were measured immediately. In addition, the influence of other coexist metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs was studied under the same condition. The concentration of Fe3+ and other coexist metal ions were finally adjusted to 200 μM, and the fluorescence intensities were recorded five minutes after the addition of BN-CDs.

The experimental group and the blank control group were measured under the same conditions at 25 °C. The fluorescence intensity was measured at 304 nm when excited at 277 nm. 1 mg of BN-CDs was first dispersed in 1 mL of ethanol, then 100 μL of these dispersed CDs solutions were transferred to a 10 mL eppendorf tube and diluted by 4.5 mL deionized water. Different amounts of Fe3+ solutions were added to the above solutions immediately and diluted to 5 mL with deionized water with the final concentrations of 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150 and 200 μM. Five minutes after Fe3+ was added, the fluorescence intensities of the mixed solutions were recorded. The fluorescence intensity of the blank control group without Fe3+ was also recorded and set as F0.

Water samples were collected from Wuhan East Lake. The water samples were first filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane and then centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was collected for Fe3+ content analysis. Fetal bovine serum sample was purchased from commercial source and processed with the following steps: 10 mL fetal bovine serum and 20 mL acetonitrile were first added into a 50 mL eppendorf tube, and then the mixture was stirred for one minute and centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was collected and stocked at 4 °C and diluted to the appropriate concentration when used for further Fe3+ content analysis.

The morphology of the obtained BN-CDs were characterized by TEM and AFM. TEM image in Fig. 1 A showed that the obtained BN-CDs were well dispersed and uniform in size. HRTEM characterization of CDs was shown in Fig. 1A (insert picture). The lattice spacing of 0.34 nm indicated a graphitic structure of BN-CDs, which was close to the (002) facet [10]. The size distribution of the BN-CDs ranged from 0.7 nm to 4.2 nm, with an average particle size of 2.4 nm (Fig. 1B). AFM image in Fig. 1C showed that the BN-CDs were monodispersed, with a particle height of approximately 1.5 nm, which was consistent with the results of TEM images. The XRD pattern of BN-CDs showed a broad peak and also some sharp peaks with the range of 17°—30° (Fig. S6), this phenomenon indicated that the BN-CDs had both graphitic structure and amorphous carbon. But, the broad band at this theta may be ascribed to either some amorphous residual organic, which was consistent with other reported CDs [11].

FT-IR and XPS were carried out to study the chemical composition of synthetic BN-CDs. In Fig. 2 A, the FT-IR peak at 3370 cm−1 attributed to the NH and OH stretching vibrations. The characteristic peaks at 1361 cm−1, 1090 cm-1 and 1020 cm−1 attributed to the BO stretching vibrations, CB stretching vibrations and BOH bending vibrations, respectively. These characteristic peaks were found both in the precursors and BN-CDs, which suggesting that the precursor had been successfully transferred into CDs. Notably, a significant absorption peak at 1738 cm−1 was found in the BN-CDs compared with the precursors, which attributed to the characteristic peaks of CO stretching vibrations in NHCOO bond. The FT-IR spectroscopy indicated that the as prepared CDs were well functionalized with NH2 group and B(OH)2 group. To further confirm this conclusion, XPS surveys were also carried out to study the chemical bonds on the survey of the BN-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, there existed four elements in the BN-CDs, with 76.82 % of C, 17.95 % of O, 3.96 % of N, and 1.27 % of B, respectively. The ratio of N to B in BN-CDs was nearly the same as their ratio in the precursors, indicating that NH2 group and boronic acid group were well transferred into the skeleton of BN-CDs. There were five peaks deconvoluted from C1s spectrum, corresponding to CC/CC at 287.7 eV, CO at 286.2 eV, CO at 288.0 eV, CN at 285.4 eV, CB at 283.9 eV [12,13] (Fig. 3B). These peaks deconvoluted from N1s spectrum at 398.3 eV, 399.2 eV, and 400.1 eV revealed the presence of pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N in the skeleton of BN-CDs [14], respectively. The B1s Spectrum could easily deconvolute into two peaks, BC at 190.1 eV, BO at 190.9 eV [13], which also revealed the presence of boronic acid group. The results from the XPS analysis were in agreement with the FT-IR results, revealing that the CDs were functioned with NH2 and boronic acid group. These results demonstrated that the BN-CDs were successfully prepared.

The optical properties of BN-CDs were characterized by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 4 ). There were two strong absorption bands at 224 nm and 247 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs, while only a weak absorption peak at about 202 nm in the spectrum of precursors. According to previous research, the absorption band at 247 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond [15,16]. The result indicated that the precursors have transformed into nanocarbon dots.

The Fluorescence spectrum showed that the BN-CDs had a maximum emission wavelength at 304 nm when excited at 277 nm (Fig. 4). When excitation wavelength changed from 246 nm to 286 nm, the maximum emission wavelength remain unchanged at 304 nm (Fig. S1). This excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior might be ascribed to the less surface defects and the more uniform particle size distributions [17,18]. It is worth noting that the quantum yield of BN-CDs (details in Fig. S7, Table S1) is as high as 31.5 % when excited at 280 nm, which is higher than many recently reported BN-CDs.

To test the photo stability, the BN-CDs was exposed under an 8 W ultraviolet lamp at 254 nm for 24 h. Fig. S2 showed that the fluorescence intensity of the BN-CDs at 304 nm kept almost unchanged during the irradiation time, indicating that the BN-CDs could remain stable when exposed to the ultraviolet light.

In order to ensure the BN-CDs can be applied in complex biological environment and natural water environment, the pH dependent fluorescence behavior of the obtained BN-CDs was also observed. As shown in Fig. 5 , the BN-CDs exhibited stable photoluminescence when pH value changed from 2 to 11, which might attribute to the NH2 and boronic acid group functioned at the surface of CDs. Such excellent pH stability indicated that the BN-CDs can be used in many extreme test environments.

We speculate that the good selectivity of BN-CDs for Fe3+ is related to the surface groups of BN-CDs. Usually, Cu2+, Pb2+ ions etc. will cause fluorescence quenching of CDs because of the amino groups on the surface. On one hand, the results of FT-IR and XPS indicated that many amino groups on the surface of the BN-CDs were converted to carbamate groups (Fig. 2). This change greatly weakened the basicity of the amino group and increased the steric hindrance around the nitrogen atom, finally impaired the coordination ability of the amino group. Thus, the ions like Cu2+ and Pb2+ could not quench BN-CDs efficiently. On the other hand, due to the presence of boric acid groups, the BN-CDs contained many dihydroxy groups on the surface. These hydroxyl groups were good ligands for Fe3+. However, the coordination ability of hydroxyl with Fe2+ is much weaker than that of Fe3+. So Fe3+ could strongly quench the fluorescence of BN-CDs while Fe2+ had little influence on the fluorescence of the BN-CDs.

Before the detection of Fe3+ in real samples, the quenching coefficient ((F0-F) / F0) versus different concentrations of Fe3+ (0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150, 200 μM) were studied first. Fig. 7 showed that the fluorescence intensity of the BN-CDs at 304 nm was gradually decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration (CFe 3+). The quenching coefficient showed a good linearity versus CFe 3+ in the liner range of 0.5–80 μM and 80–200 μM. The correlation equations were (F0-F)/F0 = 0.00768 + 0.00539 CFe 3+ (0.5–80 μM) and (F0-F)/F0 = 0.27246 + 0.00222 CFe 3+ (80–200 μM) respectively; the corresponding regression coefficient (R2, n = 6) were 0.996 (0.5–80 μM) and 0.995 (80–200 μM). It should be noted that the calculated LOD was 0.1 μM (S/N = 3), which was far less than the maximal permissible level set by United States Environmental Protection Agency [24]. These results indicated that the BN-CDs had an excellent sensitivity to Fe3+.

The good stability, excellent selectivity and high sensitivity to Fe3+ showed above indicated that the BN-CDs could be applied to detect Fe3+ in many complex samples. To confirm this, local lake water (Wuhan East Lake) and fetal bovine serum samples were chosen to verify the validity of this method, and precision was tested by standard addition technique. As shown in Table 2 , 7.23 μM of Fe3+ was found in the lake water and 762.12 μM of Fe3+ (Diluted to the appropriate concentration during the test) was found in the fetal bovine serum. It should be noted that the recovery rate was from 99.8%–106.4%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 0.4 % to 3.7 %. To further verify the accuracy of the results, the content of iron ions in East Lake samples was also measured by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Fig. S4). The AAS results showed that the content of iron ions was 7.26 μM (RSD = 9.93 %), which was consistent with the results in the above method. These results revealed that this method is very reliable for Fe3+ detection in practical samples.

In general, the fluorescence quenching process of CDs by metal ions is divided into dynamic and static quenching process. The former is caused by the interaction between the quencher and the excited state of CDs, while the latter is caused by the interaction between the quencher and the ground state of CDs. In some cases, fluorophore can not only be quenched in a dynamic process, but also can be quenched in a static quenching process with the same quencher. That is, the dynamic and static quenching occurs at the same time. According to Stern-Volmer equation, when dynamic quenching process occurred, there would be an equation:
(2) F0/F = τ 0/τ =1 + KSV[Q].
When static quenching process occurred, there would be a similar equation.
(3) F0/F = 1 + K[Q].

τ0 and τ are the fluorescent lifetime in the absence and the presence of quencher, while F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity in the absence and the presence of quencher. [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, and K is the formation constant of the quencher and fluorophore.
When dynamic and static quenching process proceeded at the same time, the Stern-Volmer graph would not be a straight line, but be a rising curve that curves to the Y axis.

The Stern-Volmer graph (F0/F versus CFe 3+) in Fig. S3 showed a rising curve that curves to the Y axis, which indicated that the quenching process was probably a dynamic and static quenching process. The most accurate way to discriminate quenching process is the measurement of fluorescence lifetime before and after the addition of quencher to CDs. In the case of static quenching, the presence of the quencher does not change the fluorescent lifetime of the excited state of CDs, then τ 0/τ = 1. If the presence of quencher will shorten the fluorescence lifetime of the excited state of CDs in dynamic quenching process, and there is a relationship between the fluorescence intensity and fluorescent lifetime:
(4)
τ 0/τ=F0/F.

As shown in Fig. 8 A, the fluorescent lifetime of BN-CDs was 1.937 ns, and got a little change to 1.819 ns after the addition of Fe3+. These results demonstrated that the quenching of BN-CDs contained a dynamic quenching process. To further verify our conjecture, the quenching efficiency of BN-CDs at different temperature was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 8B, the quenching efficiency was almost unchanged when temperature changed from 25 °C to 50 °C. This phenomenon belonged neither to a dynamic nor to a static quenching process, in which the quenching efficiency would be increased or decreased after the rising of temperature. So, the quenching process was a result of static quenching process and dynamic quenching process equilibrium.

The above investigation demonstrated that the BN-CDs were quenched by dynamic and static quenching process. The most probable quenching mechanism could be described as follows: Because BN-CDs was functionalized with boric acid groups, the B atom in the boric acid group is easy to combine with the hydroxyl group in water to form a sp3 hybrid B atom. These boric acid group contains three oxygen atoms that can coordinate with Fe3+. The Fe3+ at the interface of BN-CDs coordinated with hydroxyl groups and formed non-fluorescent complexes. When CDs were moving in the solution, Fe3+ in the solution also collided with the CDs. This dynamic and static synergy eventually quenched the fluorescence of the CDs (Fig. 8C).

In order to explore the way to improve the selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ ions, we have proposed an idea of using sp3 hybrid boric acid group to improve the selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ detection. We designed and synthesized carbon dots functionalized with boric acid groups. The prepared BN-CDs were codoped with boron and nitrogen, functioned with amounts of NH2, OH and B(OH)2 groups on the surface with very uniform size around 2.4 nm. The quantum yield of the BN-CDs was up to 31.5 % and had a good water solubility, excellent optical and pH stability. The fluorescence of BN-CDs can be quickly and selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions. The quenching efficiency of the BN-CDs showed a good liner relationship between 1–80 μM (R2 = 0.988) and 80–200 μM (R2 = 0.995), with LOD of 0.1 μM. Finally, the BN-CDs were successfully applied to sensor Fe3+ in fetal bovine serum and water samples. These excellent properties of carbon dots validate this method is successful, and can provide a reference for researchers to design and synthesize CDs for Fe3+ with high selectivity. Besides, we hope to synthesize CDs with excellent performance and suitable wavelength so that they can be applied to biological applications such as cell imaging in the future.

Hao Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing - original draft. Lan-Fang Pang: Resources, Software. Meng-Jie Fu: Software, Resources. Xiao-Feng Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing - original draft. Hong Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31670370 and 21777126, Beijing, China). The authors thank the Large-Scale Instrument and Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University for the support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
Additional information, including detailed reagents, instruments and additional figures were listed in Supplementary material.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A red carpet for iron metabolism
Cell

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two to tango: regulation of mammalian Iron metabolism
Cell
